title: Client RSSI/SNR Quality Monitoring (Wireless)
dataSources: `index=catalyst sourcetype=cisco:dnac:client`. from the `client` modular input in the Cisco Catalyst Add-on (Splunkbase 7538) polling `GET /dna/intent/api/v1/client-detail`. Default poll interval: 900s (15m). Volume: ~1 event per client per poll (can be very high volume). RBAC: NETWORK-ADMIN-ROLE. Key fields: `macAddress` (string), `hostType` (string), `connectionType` (string), `healthScore{}` (object), `ssid` (string), `vlanId` (string), `location` (string). Wireless-specific fields: `rssi`, `snr`, `ssid`, `location`.
spl: index=catalyst sourcetype="cisco:dnac:client" connectionType="WIRELESS" | stats avg(rssi) as avg_rssi avg(snr) as avg_snr count as client_count by ssid, location | eval signal_quality=case(avg_rssi>=-65,"Good",avg_rssi>=-75,"Fair",1==1,"Poor") | sort avg_rssi
description: Monitors wireless client signal quality (RSSI and SNR) by SSID and location to identify areas with poor wireless coverage.
value: Poor RSSI and SNR directly cause slow connections, dropped sessions, and user complaints. Monitoring these metrics proactively finds coverage gaps.
implementation: Enable the `client` input in the Cisco Catalyst TA pointing to `index=catalyst`. The TA polls client detail data from the Catalyst Center Intent API every 60 minutes. Key fields: `macAddress`, `hostType`, `connectionType`, `ssid`, `vlanId`, `location`, `healthScore`, `rssi`, `snr`.
visualization: Table (ssid, location, avg_rssi, avg_snr, signal_quality), heat map or geospatial chart if `location` is normalized, threshold-based alerts on Poor signal_quality.

references.title: Catalyst Center Wireless RF Profile API — Cisco DevNet

knownFalsePositives:
**High-density venue with inherently low RSSI at the cell edge.** In stadiums, convention centers, and lecture halls, AP placement is optimized for capacity rather than uniform signal strength. Clients at the cell edge may report RSSI values in the -72 to -80 dBm range, which is expected in high-density designs. Distinguish by checking whether the affected `siteId` corresponds to a known high-density venue. Suppress by maintaining a `catalyst_site_thresholds` lookup with per-site RSSI thresholds and applying site-specific alerting rather than a global threshold.

**Building construction materials attenuating signal in specific zones.** Concrete walls, metal partitions, elevator shafts, and glass facades can create RF dead zones where RSSI is consistently low without indicating an AP problem. Distinguish by checking whether the low-RSSI clients are concentrated in specific physical areas within a site. Suppress by excluding known dead zones from RSSI alerting — these require physical site survey remediation, not Splunk tuning.

**Client device with weak antenna or driver issue reporting abnormally low RSSI.** Some client device models (older laptops, IoT sensors, medical devices) have weaker antennas or driver bugs that cause them to report lower RSSI than other clients connected to the same AP. Distinguish by checking whether the low RSSI is specific to certain `clientMacAddress` values or `deviceType` categories while other clients on the same AP report normal RSSI. Suppress by filtering known weak-antenna device types from the RSSI quality dashboard.

**Outdoor AP coverage area with weather-dependent signal propagation.** Outdoor APs may show RSSI fluctuations due to weather conditions (rain attenuation, temperature-dependent propagation). Distinguish by checking whether the RSSI variation correlates with time of day or season, and whether the affected APs are outdoor models. Suppress by using wider RSSI thresholds for outdoor APs and tracking weather-correlated trends separately.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Cisco Catalyst Add-on for Splunk (Splunkbase 7538) ≥1.0 with the **client** modular input enabled, writing to `index=catalyst` with `sourcetype=cisco:dnac:client`.
• Catalyst Center **2.3.5+** with Assurance licensing for wireless client detail (RSSI, SNR).
• Wireless APs must be managed by Catalyst Center and reporting to the Assurance engine.
• RBAC: service account with **NETWORK-ADMIN-ROLE** for client health data access.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
The TA polls `GET /dna/intent/api/v1/client-detail` (or the client listing API) at the configured interval (default 900 seconds). Each wireless client event includes RF signal quality metrics.

Key fields for RSSI/SNR monitoring:
• `rssi` (integer, dBm): received signal strength indicator. Typical range: -30 dBm (excellent) to -90 dBm (unusable). Industry thresholds: ≥-65 dBm (good), -65 to -72 dBm (acceptable), -72 to -80 dBm (poor), <-80 dBm (critical).
• `snr` (integer, dB): signal-to-noise ratio. ≥25 dB is good, 15-25 dB is marginal, <15 dB is poor.
• `connectionType`: must be "WIRELESS" — wired clients do not have RSSI/SNR.
• `macAddress`: unique client identifier.
• `apName`: the AP the client is associated with — essential for identifying coverage gaps.
• `siteId`: site UUID — enables per-site RSSI analysis.
• `frequency`: "2.4" or "5.0" or "6.0" GHz band — RSSI thresholds differ by band.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert

```spl
index=catalyst sourcetype="cisco:dnac:client" connectionType="WIRELESS"
| stats avg(rssi) as avg_rssi avg(snr) as avg_snr dc(macAddress) as client_count by apName, siteId, frequency
| eval rssi_quality=case(avg_rssi>=-65, "Good", avg_rssi>=-72, "Acceptable", avg_rssi>=-80, "Poor", 1==1, "Critical")
| eval snr_quality=case(avg_snr>=25, "Good", avg_snr>=15, "Marginal", 1==1, "Poor")
| sort avg_rssi
```

Understanding this SPL:
• **`connectionType="WIRELESS"`**: Required — only wireless clients have RSSI/SNR metrics.
• **`stats avg(rssi) ... by apName, siteId, frequency`**: Averages RSSI across all clients per AP per band. This gives a per-AP coverage quality metric. APs with consistently low average RSSI have coverage issues.
• **`eval rssi_quality=case(...)`**: Industry-standard RSSI quality bands. Note: 2.4 GHz signals typically show higher RSSI than 5 GHz due to propagation characteristics — consider separate thresholds per band.
• **`eval snr_quality`**: SNR ≥25 dB is required for reliable data at high throughput rates. Below 15 dB, clients experience significant packet loss.

Step 3 — Validate
• **UI comparison:** Open Catalyst Center > Assurance > Health > Client Health > [select a wireless client]. Compare the RSSI and SNR values with the Splunk results for the same client and time window.
• **AP-level check:** Select an AP in Catalyst Center > Assurance > Device 360. Compare the average client RSSI shown in Device 360 with the per-AP average in Splunk.
• **Band comparison:** Run the search filtered by `frequency="2.4"` and `frequency="5.0"` separately. 5 GHz clients should generally show lower RSSI (shorter range) but similar SNR.

Step 4 — Operationalize
• **Dashboard:** Heatmap of average RSSI by AP (green/yellow/red), histogram of RSSI distribution, and a table of APs with "Poor" or "Critical" average RSSI.
• **Alert:** Trigger when an AP's average RSSI drops below -80 dBm with >5 clients — indicates a coverage gap affecting multiple users. Throttle per `apName` for 4 hours to avoid repeated alerts for persistent coverage issues.
• **Site survey trigger:** APs consistently showing "Poor" RSSI should be flagged for physical site survey to verify AP placement, transmit power, and antenna orientation.

Step 5 — Troubleshoot
• **RSSI values all appear as zero or null:** The `client` input may not be collecting detailed wireless metrics. Check whether the Catalyst Center API version supports RSSI in the client listing endpoint.
• **Average RSSI misleading for high-density venues:** In stadiums or lecture halls, the average RSSI is expected to be lower (many clients at cell edge). Use per-site thresholds from a `catalyst_site_thresholds` lookup.
• **SNR values not populated:** Some Catalyst Center versions or AP models do not report SNR in the client API. Use RSSI alone for quality assessment in this case.
• **No client events arriving:** verify the `client` input is enabled and check `index=_internal` for API error or endpoint timeout messages from the TA.
• **NULL or zero values in health scores:** some per-client fields (RSSI, SNR) are only populated for wireless clients; empty values for wired clients are expected.
• **More events than expected:** the client API generates one event per connected client per poll; if credential access is denied, check the service account role.
grandmaExplanation: We check the wireless signal strength for every connected device and flag areas where the signal is too weak for a good experience.
